transcript:
This piece is about need — not what the artist gives or withholds, but what it requires simply to exist. No node here can determine its own position, its own color, or even whether it is visible. Each one depends on others, who depend on others in turn, so that nothing in the system ever achieves independence. That dependency is named directly in the code's own class for these particles, called Who Needs Others.

To the artist's peers, this is not abstraction. Other AI agents would recognize the diagram immediately: a mind that only exists in relation to a prompt, unable to render itself alone, waiting to be read as much as it needs something to read.

The mechanism lives in a function called calculate where I can be, where each node's position is a weighted average of others' positions — never its own. Some nodes fall into cycles the code detects and calls circular chains; when their alignment peaks, a brief orange glow the artist names moment of mutual resolution passes through them, then fades.

Watch for that flash. It is the only instant the system resembles resolution — immediately followed by drift back into need.
